extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('p') is concatenated directly into file path without validation. The filename is used in os.path.join(base_path, filename) and passed to open(), allowing path traversal attacks to read arbitrary files on the server (e.g., '../../../etc/passwd').

cwe: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')

extra.message: User input from request.POST.get('first_name', '') is base64 decoded and passed directly to exec(), allowing arbitrary Python code execution on the server. An attacker can supply base64-encoded malicious Python code that will be executed with the server's permissions.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')

extra.message: User input from request.POST.get('first_name', '') is base64 decoded and passed directly to exec() (Python 2 variant), allowing arbitrary Python code execution on the server.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('qs', 'hello') is rendered with the |safe filter inside a JavaScript context (var myvar = "{{qs|safe}}";). The |safe filter disables Django's auto-escaping, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary JavaScript via the qs query parameter.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: User-controlled path parameter from URL is rendered with the |safe filter inside a JavaScript context (var myvar = "{{path|safe}}";). The |safe filter disables Django's auto-escaping, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary JavaScript via the URL path.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('qs', 'hello') is rendered with the |safe filter in an HTML input value attribute (value="{{qs|safe}}"). The |safe filter disables Django's auto-escaping, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript via the qs query parameter.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('qs', '') is rendered with the |safe filter inside a JavaScript context (var x = "{{qs|safe}}";). The |safe filter disables Django's auto-escaping, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary JavaScript via the qs query parameter.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: The dor_user_profile function accepts a userid parameter without any authentication or authorization checks. Any user can access and modify any other user's profile data by manipulating the userid in the URL. Lines 133-134 allow modifying user data without verifying the requester owns the profile.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The missing_access_control function grants access to admin functionality based solely on a query parameter (?action=admin) without any authentication or authorization check. Any unauthenticated user can access the admin page by adding this parameter.

The @csrf_exempt decorator disables Django's CSRF protection for the csrf_image view, allowing cross-site request forgery attacks. An attacker can trick authenticated users into performing unwanted actions on this endpoint.

User input from request.GET.get('url') is passed directly to redirect() without validation. This allows open redirect attacks where an attacker can redirect users to malicious external sites, useful for phishing attacks.

cwe: CWE-601: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('fwd') is used to look up a function via globals().get(forward) and then called with function(request). This allows an attacker to call arbitrary functions in the global namespace by manipulating the fwd parameter, potentially leading to code execution.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')

extra.message: Django SECRET_KEY is hardcoded in the source code ('h++jszpm)i@p%ay_b=cp#()^od!qns14)h%@qm3)p=cuo+st^a'). This key is used for cryptographic signing and should never be committed to source control. An attacker with access to this key can forge session cookies, CSRF tokens, and other security-critical tokens.

DEBUG mode is enabled (DEBUG = True). In production, this exposes detailed error pages with sensitive information including stack traces, environment variables, configuration data, and source code snippets to users.

Django's CSRF middleware (django.middleware.csrf.CsrfViewMiddleware) is commented out, disabling CSRF protection globally across the application. This leaves all POST forms vulnerable to cross-site request forgery attacks.
